content.full_text:
Fern Polrrrcel PRACTrcss CoMMrssroN
-i28 J Street . Suite 620 . Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
April 15,2008
Harold C. Williams
City Hall
701 E. Carson Street
Carson, California 907 49
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.I-08-052
Dear Mr. Williams
This letter responds to yollr reqLlest for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Retbrm Act (the "Act").1 This letter is based on the facts
presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as
tlre finder of fact when it renders advice. (ln re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Also,
please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore
offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as
common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. We also offer no
opinion on the issue of simultaneously holding positions with two or more public
agencies as governed under Govemment Code Section 1099.
QUESTION
Do you have a disqualifying conflict of interest that would prevent you from
participating in city council decisions that would af'fect the West Basin Municipal Water
District (the "WBMWD")?
CONCLUSION
No. Based on the limited facts you provided, you do not have a disqualifying
conflict of interest because the "public generally" exception applies to exempt you from
disqualification.
I The Political Refirrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 9l0l.t. All
statutory ret'erences are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. I'he regulations of the Fair
Rrlitical Practices Commission are contained in Sections I8I I0 through Itt997 of T'itle 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory relerences are to Title 2. Divisitln 6 ol the Califil'nia Code of
Regulations. unless otherwise indicated.


File No. I-08-052
Page No.2
FACTS
You are a city council member for the City of Carson (the "City"). You also have
been offered a position to serve as an independent contractor for WBMWD and under
that contract, you will serve as a consultant and communicate with the public and other
cities about the WBMWD and its services. The WBMWD is a public agency that
purchases imported water from the Metropolitan Water District of Southern Califomia.
fn" WpUWD wholesales water to cities and other water purveyors and also provides
educational outreach regarding water conservation and prevention of storm drain
pollution. The WBMWD also sells water to the City through a private purveyor. Yott
also informed us that your work for WBMWD is not covered under WBMWD's conflict
of interest code.
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interest of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)
Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
A public official has a "financial interest" in a govemmental decision, within the
meaning of the Act, when it is "reasonably foreseeable" that the governmental decision
will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic
interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) In order to determine whether a public
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision, the
Commission has adopted an eighrstep analytical framework. (Regulation 18700(bX1)-
(8).) The general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public
official makes a governmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on one or more of his or her financial interests. (Section 87103.)
Step One: Are You a Public Official?
City council members are public officials under the Act. (See Section 84048(a).)
Step Two: Will You Be Making, Participating in Making, or Influencing a
Governmental Decision?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the purview of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.)
In addition, a public official "participates in making" a govemmental decision
when he or she, without substantive review, negotiates, advises, or makes
recommendations regarding a decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) You will be participating
in making a governmental decision if you advise, recommend or negotiate a course of
action for the City.


File No. I-08-052
Page No. 3
Furthermore, a public official is "influencing a govemmental decision" if he or
she contacts, or appears before, or otherwise irttempts to influence any member, officer,
employee, or consultant of the government agency regarding the decisions. (Regulation
18702.3.) Therefore, you would be influencing a'governmental decision if you contact,
appear before, or otherwise attempt to influence any member of the Carson city cotrncil
regarding govemmental decisions.
You have stated that there is currently not a governmental decision before the city
council that pertains to the WBMWD. Should such a decision arise, you must look
carefully at each decision and apply the eight-step analysis described herein.
Step Three: What Are Your Economic Interests?
A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision within the
meaning of the Act if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on
the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated
economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The applicable economic
interests include:
1. 
An interest in a business entity in which a public
official has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more.
(Section 87103(a), Regulation 18703.1(a).) An interest in any
business entity2 in which a public official is a director, officer,
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management. (Section 87103(d), Regulation 18703. 1(b).)
2. 
An interest in real property in which a public official
has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section
87 103(b), Regulation 187 03.2.)
3. 
Any source of income, including promised income, to
the public official that aggregates to $500 or more within 12
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c), Regulation
18703.3.)
4. 
Any source of gifts to the public official if the gifts
aggregate to $390 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision. (Section 87103(e), Regulation I8703.4.)
5. 
A public official also has an economic interest in his or
her personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as
those of his or her immediate family. This is also known as
the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103,
Regulation 18703.5.)
: Section 82005 provides: -'Business entity" means any organization or enterprise operated fbr
profit. including but not limited to a proprietorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint venture.
syndicate. corporation, or association. This detlnition does not include nonprofits or governmental entities


File No. I-08-052
Page No.4
Economic Interests Identified
Source of Income: Your question focuses on whether the income you receive
from the WBMWD creates a potential conflict with your position on the city council. A
public official has an economic interest in his or her sources of income. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18703.3.) While the Act's definition of income excludes "salary . . . received
from a state, local, or federal government agency," (Section 82030(b)(2)), that exception
does not apply when, as in your capacity with WBMWD, an individual receives income
from a public agency but in that capacity is not a "public official" as defined by the Act
and Commission regulations. The Act defines "public official" as "every member,
officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local govemment agency." (Section 82048.)
Regulation 18701(a) defines when an individual who independently contracts with a
public agency is a "consllltant" under the Act and therefore a public official under
Section 82048.
The term "consultant" under the Act refers to a particular set of individuals with
particular duties, and decision-making responsibilities. (See, for example, our analysis in
Fitzpatrick Advice Letter, I-05-198, copy enclosed.) WBMWD has not designated your
position as a "consultant" under its conflict of interest code and you will not file a
statement of economic interests for this position. Consequently, the salary you receive
from WBMWD will not be subject to the "government salary exception" to the definition
of income. Therefore, you have a source of income economic interest in the payments
you receive from WBMWD under your contract with that agency.
Personal Finances; Every public official has an economic interest in his or her
personal finances and those of his or her immediate family. (Section 87103; Regulation
18703.5.) A govemmental decision will have an effect on this economic interest if the
decision will result in your or your immediate family's personal expenses, income, assets
or liabilities, other than the financial effect on your real property interest or business,
increasing or decreasing. (lbid.) You have not provided any facts to suggest your
participation in any governmental decision would affect your personal finances in any
way. We do not, therefore, further analyze your personal finances as a potential conflict
of interest.
We restrict our analysis only to the question of whether you may participate in
decisions as a city councilmember affecting your source of income, the WBMWD.
Step Four: Are your interests directly or indirectly involved in the decision?
A source of income is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency
when that person, either directly or by an agent:
(l) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request; or
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding conceming the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject
of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person. (Regulation 18704.1(a).)


File No. I-08-052
Page No. 5
You will have an economic interest in WBMWD as a source of income. Thus, if
WBMWD is a named party in, or the initiator or subject of, the proceedings in which the
decisions will occur, WBMWD will be directly involved in the decision. Otherwise, it
will be indirectly involved. (See Regulation 18704.1(b).) You have not provided
examples of decisions that would come before the city council. Yor.r will need to analyze
each decision on a decision-by decision basis.
Step Five: What is the applicable materiality standard?
Generally, any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source
of income to a public official, and who is directly involved in a decision before the
official's agency is deemed material. This standard for determining materiality is known
as the "one penny" rllle. If the entity is affected by even one penny, the financial effect is
deemed material. (See Regulation 18705.3(a).)
The materiality standards for indirectly involved sources of income that are non-
profit entities, including government agencies, are set forth in regulation 18705.3(bX2)
(copy enclosed), and depend on the gross annual receipts of the entity.
Step Six: Is the financial effect reasonably foreseeable?
A material financial effect on an economic interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if
it is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards will be met as a
result of the govemmental decision. (Regulation 18706(a).) An effect need not be
certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (ln re Thorner, I FPPC Ops. 198 (1975).) On the other hand, if an effect is
only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
Again, you must apply this standard to each applicable decision.
Step Seven: Public Generally Exception
Under the "public generally" exception, an official may still participate in a
decision despite a conflict of interest if the effect of the decision on the official's interest
is not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Section 87103.)
Regulation l87O7explains that the exception will apply if the decision will affect a
significant segment of the public generally in substantially the same manner as it would
affect the public official's interests. Regulation 18707 .1(b)( lXD) states that decisions
that affect a federal, state, or local government which the public official has an economic
interest, such as WBMWD, the decision will affect all members of the public under the
jurisdiction of that governmental entity.
The comment at the end of Regulation 18707.1 provides: "The term'affect all
members of the public' is intended to cover decisions affecting the public in general but
to exclude decisions which uniquely benefit a public official."
Based upon these provisions, you will have to analyze each decision before the
city council to determine whether it will "uniquely affect" you and your position at
WBMWD. If you are not uniquely affected (e.g., the decision does not pertain to your
contract with, or income from WBMWD, then the public generally exception applies and
you may participate in the decision.


File No. I-08-052
Pa-ee No. 6
Step Eight: Legal Required Exception
The "legally required participation" mle applies when the official's participation
in a governmental decision is legally required. (Section 87101; Regulation 18708.) You
have not presented any facts that pertain to this exception. We therefore do not address
it.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
[\rr'gt{^ Al"
By: Heather M. Rowan
Counsel, Legal Division
HMR:jgl
Enclosures
